Title: Generic .tres mapper
Summary: How Bleepforge writes .tres files for any manifest-declared domain — handler dispatch table, override registry for FoB's bespoke writers, sub-resource reconciliation, ext-resource minting, AtlasTexture preservation. Built in v0.2.7; production dispatch lands in v0.2.9.
Body:
## The shape

Every `.tres` save in Bleepforge goes through one funnel: `writeTres(domain, entity, ctx)` in [server/src/internal/tres/generic/writer.ts](https://github.com/ymoraviadev-droid/bleepforge/blob/main/server/src/internal/tres/generic/writer.ts).

The dispatcher consults the **override registry** first. FoB's seven hand-tuned writers register as overrides at module init — they keep running unchanged for the seven hardcoded domains (item / quest / karma / npc / faction / dialog / balloon). When the override matches, the bespoke writer handles the save and the generic path is skipped.

When no override matches, the dispatcher falls through to the **generic path** — a manifest-driven writer composed of 12 field-type handlers. Today no FoB domain exercises this path in production (FoB retains all seven overrides through the genericization arc and ports at v0.3.0). The generic path is validated byte-identical against the real FoB karma corpus (6/6); production wiring lands when v0.2.8's importer + v0.2.9's edit UI complete the round-trip.

## The 12 handlers

Each handler covers one manifest field type. Uniform signature so the orchestrator dispatches via a flat lookup table rather than runtime branching:

```ts
type FieldHandler = (
  jsonValue: unknown,
  fieldDef: FieldDef,
  section: Section,
  propName: string,
  ctx: WriterContext,
) => string | null;
```

Return value:
- a serialized raw value → orchestrator updates / inserts the property line.
- `null` → property line is removed (or stays absent).

The array + subresource handlers are wider than this signature can express (they mutate the doc by inserting / removing sub_resource sections), so they're special-cased in the orchestrator's dispatch loop before the scalar table lookup.

**Scalar handlers** (in `handlers/`):

- `string` / `multiline` / `flag` (all in `string.ts`) — same Godot quoted-string form. Default-aware emit: empty (or matching `default`) → omit the line.
- `int` / `float` (both in `numeric.ts`) — bare-number form. Float adds the `.0` suffix for whole numbers to match Godot's int-vs-float disambiguation.
- `bool` — `true` / `false`. Defaults to `false`.
- `enum` — emits the int index (per Godot's enum serialization). The manifest's `values: string[]` provides the name → index mapping. Default = `values[0]` when not declared (Godot omits enum int 0 by convention).

**Reference handlers** (mint ext_resources):

- `ref` (`ref.ts`) — cross-domain Resource reference. Resolves the target via `WriterContext.resolveRef(domain, key)`, mints `type="Resource"` ext_resources (matches FoB's serialization convention for scripted Resource refs), dedups identical targets.
- `texture` (`texture.ts`) — Texture2D. Two non-obvious cases: **AtlasTexture preservation** (empty JSON keeps an existing `SubResource(AtlasTexture)` line verbatim — Bleepforge doesn't author atlases, so blowing them away on save would destroy user work) and **swap-drop-orphan** (non-empty JSON replacing an AtlasTexture also removes the now-orphaned AtlasTexture sub_resource so its inner atlas ext_resource can be cleaned up).
- `scene` (`scene.ts`) — PackedScene. Accepts either a `res://` URI (FoB JSON convention) or an absolute path under godotRoot. UID via `ctx.resolveSceneUid`.

**Composite handlers** (mutate the doc):

- `array` (`array.ts`) — two flavors discriminated by manifest fields:
  - `array.of: "SubResourceName"` → sub-resource arrays. Uses `reconcileSubResourceArray` for stable-identity matching via `_subId`: add / update / remove / reorder all work without churn. Typed container emits `Array[ExtResource("scriptId")]([...])`; untyped emits `[...]`. Script ext_resource for new sub_resource sections comes from `scriptIndex`.
  - `array.itemRef: { to: "domain" }` → ref arrays. Mints `type="Resource"` ext_resources, dedups identical refs. Typed container is always `Array[Object](...)` (NOT the target's script — confirmed against FoB's NPC `CasualRemarks` shape).
- `subresource` (`subresource.ts`) — single inline sub_resource (NPC `LootTable` shape). Three states: empty JSON + no existing → noop; empty JSON + existing → **recursive teardown** (also removes nested sub_resources the wrapper referenced — without this, deleting a LootTable would leave its LootEntry sections orphaned); non-empty JSON + existing → reconcile in place; non-empty JSON + no existing → mint a fresh sub_resource section.

## scriptIndex

When the array or subresource handler mints a new sub_resource section, it needs the underlying script's `uid` + `res://` path to populate the `script = ExtResource(...)` line. The **scriptIndex** singleton at [server/src/lib/scriptIndex/](https://github.com/ymoraviadev-droid/bleepforge/tree/main/server/src/lib/scriptIndex) walks every `.cs` file under the Godot project root at boot and pairs each with its `.cs.uid` sidecar (Godot 4.4+ emits these). Class name → script lookup, then `findOrAddExtResource` either reuses an existing Script ext_resource declaration or mints a new one.

Cheap (~5ms for FoB's 80 `.cs` files at boot). No watcher hook in v0.2.7 — `.cs` files don't churn during a Bleepforge editing session.

## Resolver injection

The handlers don't read from global singletons directly. Instead `WriterContext` carries five resolver callbacks the caller populates before invoking the orchestrator:

```ts
interface WriterContext {
  godotRoot: string;
  doc: Doc;
  warnings: string[];
  resolveRef: (domain, key) => { uid, resPath } | null;
  resolveTextureUid: (absPath) => string | null;
  resolveSceneUid: (resPathOrAbsPath) => string | null;
  resolveScriptByClassName: (className) => { resPath, uid } | null;
  subResources: Map<string, SubResource>;
}
```

Production wiring: resolvers are backed by `projectIndex` + `scriptIndex` + sidecar reads. Smoke tests stub them to drive specific code paths without touching the global singletons. Keeps the handlers pure-function shaped (input → output) even though the underlying lookups need disk access.

## Validation

Four smoke suites in `server/src/internal/tres/generic/`:

- `scalarsSmoke.ts` — 17 cases covering string / multiline / flag / int / float / bool / enum + default-aware emit + `showWhen` gating.
- `refsSmoke.ts` — 14 cases for ref / texture / scene, including AtlasTexture preservation + swap-drop-orphan.
- `arraysSmoke.ts` — 12 cases for sub-resource arrays (with `_subId` matching) + ref arrays, typed/untyped containers.
- `subresourceSmoke.ts` — 6 cases for the NPC LootTable shape including recursive teardown.

Plus the end-to-end validation gate:

- `genericKarmaHarness.ts` — runs the generic mapper against every `.tres` in the FoB karma corpus (6 files) using a hand-authored manifest entry that mirrors what godot-lib would emit if KarmaImpact + KarmaDelta were ported to BleepforgeRegistry. Compares emitted output byte-for-byte. 6/6 clean.

Run via `pnpm --filter @bleepforge/server smoke:generic{,-refs,-arrays,-subresource}` + `pnpm --filter @bleepforge/server harness:generic-karma`.

## What's deferred

- **Production dispatch through the generic path.** Today every FoB save hits its override; the generic path runs in tests only. v0.2.9 wires the dispatcher's fallthrough through edit UI + writeTresGeneric.
- **Generic importer** (`.tres → JSON`). v0.2.8 — mirror architecture to the writer. After importer ships, a round-trip harness validates parse → import → write → emit byte-identical.
- **One-off legacy migrations** stay in overrides forever (NPC `CasualRemark` → `CasualRemarks` is the only FoB instance). New users designing schemas don't have legacy baggage; the generic mapper deliberately doesn't run migrations.

- [Manifest-driven discovery](/help/under-the-hood/manifest-discovery) — the read side of the generic pipeline: projectIndex extension + the discovery endpoints + the UI.
- [.tres is truth, JSON is cache](/help/under-the-hood/tres-is-truth) — the data model the generic mapper writes against.
